history.label: 9th century
history.text: The Norse Viking Helgi magri (the slim) Eyvindarson originally settled the area in the 9th century.
The first mention of Akureyri is in court records from 1562, when a woman was sentenced there for adultery.

history.label: 17th century
history.text: In the 17th century, Danish merchants based their camps at the current site of Akureyri, which was one of the numerous spits of land in Pollurinn.
Permanent settlement at Akureyri started in 1778, and eight years later, the town was granted its municipal charter by the king, Christian VII, along with five other towns in Iceland.
It lost its municipal status in 1836, regaining it only in 1862.
On 1 December 1940, 'A' and 'B' flights ceased operating from Norwegian bases, but 'C' flight continued to fly Northrop N-3PBs from Akureyri until 5 April 1943.
The squadron, which was formed on 25 April 1941, flew Northrop N-3PB bombers: 'A' flight was based at RAF Reykjavik, 'B' flight at Akureyri and 'C' flight at Budareyri (Reyðarfjörður now).
The University of Akureyri was founded in 1987 and is growing rapidly.

history.label: 21st century
history.text: In the early 21st century, fishing industries have become more important in Akureyri as two of the major fishing companies of Iceland have become a more important source of revenue and are expected to.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: From then on, Akureyri grew because of the excellent port conditions and perhaps more because of the productive agricultural region around it.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: During World War II, Akureyri was one of three air bases used by the Norwegian-British No.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: 330 Squadron RNoAF also operated Catalina flying boats from Akureyri, which protected convoys between the United States, the United Kingdom, and Murmansk in northern Russia from attack by German submarines.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Under the German plan for invasion of Iceland, Operation Ikarus, Akureyri was scheduled as one of two ports at which the Germans were to land.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Jón Sveinsson, a popular author of children's books, grew up in Akureyri; his childhood home is now a museum.
